meta.manuscript_location: Shrine of the Book, Israel Museum, Jerusalem

preamble.summary: Isaiah 54 follows the Suffering Servant passage with a burst of joy — 'Sing, O barren one, who did not bear!' The chapter has 17 verses in columns XLIV-XLV. After the intensity of chapter 53, this chapter celebrates the restoration that the servant's suffering makes possible. Variants are mostly minor.
preamble.notable_variants: Verse 1: the scroll reads 'Sing, O barren one' identically. Verse 7: a minor variant in the time expression. Verse 10: the 'covenant of peace' (berit shelomi) is identical in both texts. Verse 13: 'all your children shall be taught by the LORD' — identical (quoted in John 6:45). Verse 17: a minor variant in the suffix form.
preamble.scroll_condition: Well preserved. The transition from the Suffering Servant to the celebration of restoration is fully legible.

variant_notes: Identical consonantal text. 'Sing, O barren one, who did not bear; break forth into singing!' — the joyful imperative after the servant's suffering is the same in both traditions (cf. Galatians 4:27).
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.
Plene spelling with vav. Same word.
1QIsaiah-a uses the -ki suffix characteristic of Qumran. Same meaning — 'your Maker is your husband.'
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.

1QIsaiah-a uses plene spelling of qaton (with vav). 'For a brief moment I forsook you, but with great compassion I will gather you.' The theological tension between divine abandonment and restoration is preserved identically.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.
Identical reading. 'My covenant of peace shall not be removed' — the everlasting covenant language is the same in both texts. This is one of the great covenant promises of the Hebrew Bible.
Plene spelling. Same word — 'O afflicted one, storm-tossed.'
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.

Identical reading. 'All your children shall be taught by the LORD' — quoted by Jesus in John 6:45 ('It is written in the Prophets: They will all be taught by God'). The scroll confirms this reading in the pre-Christian text.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.

1QIsaiah-a uses the characteristic Qumran -ki suffix for the second-person feminine singular. 'No weapon formed against you shall prosper' — one of the most quoted promises in Isaiah, identical in both traditions.
